explicit_text: I was born and raised in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, specifically on Hillsborough Street in an area known as Windy Hill. Growing up there in the 1940s and 50s, the community was mostly black homeowners surrounded by white neighborhoods, which shaped much of our daily life. Chapel Hill was unique in that people of similar economic status tended to live together, and despite racial tensions, there were friendships across racial lines. The town had its share of challenges, including KKK intimidation, but also strong community bonds through schools, churches, and local centers.

explicit_text: I attended Northside Elementary School in Chapel Hill, where in my early years we had so many students that classes were held in shifts—half the students in the morning and half in the afternoon. Despite the shortened school days, our teachers were dedicated and had high expectations for us, often making home visits to keep parents informed about our progress. This close relationship between teachers and families helped create a supportive learning environment during my elementary education.

entity: Lincoln High School (Chapel Hill, North Carolina)
explicit_text: After elementary school, I walked about two and a half miles to Lincoln High School on Merritt Mill Road, since there was no school bus service for us. Lincoln was more than just a school; under Principal C.A. McDougle, who acted like a surrogate father, it was a home away from home with firm but fair discipline and a strong focus on education. Despite limited resources, including secondhand books from the white Chapel Hill High School, we excelled academically and athletically, winning two state football championships during my time there in the late 1950s and early 1960s.

explicit_text: Franklin Street was a significant landmark in my daily life as it was where Chapel Hill High School was located, and I had to pass by it every day walking to Lincoln High School. Seeing the white high school on Franklin Street made me wonder if it would be better to attend there instead of walking all the way to Lincoln. Franklin Street represented the racial and educational divide in Chapel Hill during my youth in the 1950s.

explicit_text: Clark Egerton was the band director at Lincoln High School during my time there, and under his leadership, our band was one of the best in the state. Along with Mr. Bell, he helped us achieve great recognition whenever we performed. His dedication contributed to a strong sense of pride and excellence in our school's extracurricular programs during the early 1960s.

entity: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
explicit_text: After graduating from Lincoln High School, I attended North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University on a football scholarship, where I earned my BS degree. While at A&T in the early 1960s, I was actively involved in the civil rights movement, including playing a role in the 1962-63 Woolworth's sit-in boycott. It was also there that I played football alongside Jesse Jackson, which was a significant part of my college experience.

Our shared experiences on the field and in activism helped shape my commitment to social justice.

explicit_text: I was actively involved in the Woolworth's sit-in boycott in 1962-63 while I was a student at North Carolina A&T. This event was a pivotal moment in the civil rights movement in Greensboro and beyond, aiming to desegregate public spaces. Participating in this protest alongside fellow students, including Jesse Jackson, was a defining experience that connected my education with the fight for equality.

explicit_text: As a child growing up in Chapel Hill, I often went to the Hargraves Community Center during the spring and summer for recreation. It was a place where kids from the neighborhood could gather and play, supplementing the time we spent playing in the streets and open fields like Windy Hill. The community center was an important resource that helped strengthen our community ties.

explicit_text: Carrboro was the town where Lyons Park was located, the site where Lincoln High School played its football games. We often had large crowds at Lyons Park, including many white spectators, which showed the community's support for our team despite the racial divides of the time. Playing in Carrboro during the late 1950s and early 1960s was a highlight of my high school sports experience.

I remember the pride we felt playing there and the strong community support we received during the late 1950s and early 1960s.

explicit_text: Growing up in Chapel Hill, the Ku Klux Klan was a constant threat in the background, often expressing their hatred openly by writing intimidating messages like 'Get the hell out, niggers' on the roads. This was a common practice during my childhood in the 1940s and 50s, creating an environment of fear and hostility for black families like mine. Despite this, our community remained strong and resilient in the face of such extremism.

entity: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
explicit_text: My father worked as a janitor at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, which was part of our family life growing up in the town. The university was a significant institution in Chapel Hill, and although I did not attend UNC, its presence was felt throughout the community. My family's connection to the university was through my father's employment during the mid-20th century.

explicit_text: Although I did not attend Hillside High School, it was mentioned as a point of reference during my interview. Hillside High School in Durham was another historically black high school in North Carolina, similar in some ways to Lincoln High School in Chapel Hill. It represented part of the broader context of segregated education in the state during my youth.
